title: From Behind the Scenes to Building My Own Client: A Video Editor’s Honest Journey
description:
For years, I worked for clients I could never truly call my own.  
I edited videos, solved problems, stayed up late fixing projects, and helped other people grow their businesses — but almost none of the work belonged to me. Most of it was done under other people’s names, and I couldn’t even use much of it in a portfolio.

At some point, I realized that if I kept waiting for the perfect moment, I would stay invisible forever.
author: Amin

blocks.content:
So I built a portfolio from whatever I could. Old edits, personal projects, anything that could prove I was capable of doing the work. Then I started cold messaging businesses.

My offer was simple:  
“I’ll make you a free short-form video so you can see how I work. And if it’s not useful for you, I’d appreciate it if you passed my name to someone who might need it.”

Most messages went nowhere. Some people liked the idea but didn’t even have content ready. Others never replied.

Then one day, while I was on my way to the hospital, I checked my phone and saw a message:  
“I’d like to have a meeting.”

I remember staring at the screen thinking:  
\*Wait… this is a real person. A real CEO.\*

I genuinely couldn’t believe it.  
That entire day, I couldn’t focus on anything else. I kept asking myself:  
“Is this it? Did I finally do it? Could this be the beginning of a real life after all the years I spent trying?”

### First Conflict

The moment Greg agreed to a meeting, I became obsessed with proving myself.

I wanted to show that I wasn’t just another editor sending random cold messages online. So before the meeting even happened, I studied his brand, his content, and his company.

One thing stood out to me: His brand followed a five-step process, and the word “POWER” had five letters in his brand name **POWER100**.  

So I spent days creating a custom logo animation for him. The first letters were shaped like five stairs, and a ball used those steps to climb upward before the stairs transformed into the full word.  

To me, it represented growth, discipline, and progress.

I thought it showed creativity. I thought it showed initiative. I thought it showed how seriously I took the opportunity.

Two nights before our meeting, I uploaded the animation to LinkedIn and tagged Greg as a surprise.

Then I checked my phone.  
The meeting was canceled.

My heart dropped.

Greg commented respectfully and appreciated the effort, but he explained that what I did crossed a professional boundary.  

And honestly… he was right.

I remember sitting there asking myself:  
“Did my hard work just ruin my future?”

For hours, I felt completely defeated. Like I had destroyed the biggest opportunity of my life because I was too inexperienced to understand professional limits.

I sent him a message explaining my intention. Thankfully, he decided to keep the meeting as long as I removed the post.

The meeting itself went well. We talked about my background, the type of work they needed, pricing, and he asked me to give him 5 shorts of an event to start — and I delivered them quickly.

But I made another mistake:  
I underestimated the workload.  

I gave a very low price because I wanted the opportunity more than anything. At the time, I didn’t fully understand how large the project really was.

Still, things kept moving. Another meeting was scheduled. I even introduced my brother to Greg and the developer during one of the calls.  

For the first time in my life, it genuinely felt like something real was finally starting.

I sent over a contract, even though I had never done that before and barely knew what I was doing. I was trying to act professionally while secretly figuring everything out as I went.

But progress was slow. There were long gaps between meetings. It took almost two months after the first meeting before I could properly begin the actual project.

And then everything became harder.

### When Everything Went Wrong

Right when the project finally started, major protests broke out in Iran.  

The government shut down the internet almost completely. Not just social media. Everything.  

No messaging. No calls. No communication.

For days, I had absolutely no way to contact Greg or Ray.

At the time, I had already promised to deliver 10 shorts within a month.

When the internet partially returned, it only worked weakly at around 4 AM using VPN configurations that barely connected.  

The project files were around 40 GB. The download speed was painfully slow.  

So every night, while most people were asleep, I stayed awake trying to download footage minute by minute.  

It took me  five sleepless nights  just to get the files.

And after finally starting the edits, I realized the workload of these 10 highlight shorts was much bigger than I expected.  

The videos needed both vertical and horizontal formats. The subtitles had to be completely re-synced. The horizontal format required different subtitle layouts. The cuts, lower thirds, fonts, and timing all needed adjustments.  

On top of that, nobody had provided the guest names or job titles, so I had to research and find them myself.

At the same time, I still had hospital responsibilities and real-life pressure outside the project.

But I refused to let the opportunity fail.

I kept pushing because I wanted them to love working with me. I wanted to prove that choosing me was the right decision.

So I forced myself forward with almost no sleep, constant stress, and an overwhelming fear of disappointing the people who had trusted me.

Eventually, after delays and several revision rounds, I delivered the project.

But even then, the pressure didn’t stop.

I had already completed \*\*16 shorts\*\* before receiving any payment. The LinkedIn connection still hadn’t been accepted. The contract hadn’t really been acknowledged. And although nobody treated me badly, I constantly worried that everything could disappear at any moment.

Then came another major problem: \*\*Payment\*\*.  

Because of sanctions against Iran, transferring money became complicated.

I remember sitting in that meeting watching the concern on Greg’s face, realizing that even after all the work, something completely outside my control could still ruin everything.

I tried to explain that working together was still possible and that there were alternative ways to transfer payments.

Then I waited.  
And worried.

Thankfully, a few days later, Ray messaged me and helped solve the payment issue.

For the first time in my life, I finally received real money from an international client I had found completely on my own.

I thought maybe the hard part was finally over.  
I was wrong.

###  War, Silence, and Uncertainty

I completed the next set of 10 shorts and started discussing more realistic pricing for future projects.

Then the war started.  

And once again, the internet disappeared.

This time, I had already delivered the videos, but there were still revisions waiting. Ray sent feedback, and right before I could respond, the connection was gone again.  

Completely.

For over a week, I tried reaching Greg through a friend because I no longer had stable access myself.  

Nothing.

Then weeks passed. Then months.  

No updates. No communication. No certainty about whether the opportunity still existed.

Every day felt like watching something important slowly slip away while being unable to do anything about it.

And the hardest part was knowing that from the outside, it might simply look like I disappeared.  

Not because I stopped caring. Not because I became irresponsible. But because I was trapped behind circumstances I couldn’t control.

###  What This Experience Really Taught Me

Maybe, in the end, this story looks like a failure.  
Maybe this was the opportunity that slipped through my hands.

But that’s not how I see it anymore.

Because before Greg, I was just someone editing behind the scenes for other people.  

This project changed that.

For the first time, I found a real client on my own. I held meetings. I negotiated. I created ideas. I handled revisions. I dealt with pressure, mistakes, deadlines, payment problems, internet shutdowns, sanctions, and uncertainty.

And despite everything, I kept trying to move forward.

So even if this collaboration never fully became what I hoped it would become, it still changed my life.

Because it proved something important to me:  

I wasn’t just someone waiting for an opportunity anymore.
I was finally someone capable of creating one.

About a month later, Ray finally replied to one of my messages. He told me that my payment was safe and that my security mattered more.  

Honestly, that message meant a lot to me. In the middle of all the uncertainty, stress, and silence, it genuinely gave me comfort.

And despite everything that happened throughout this story, I’m still grateful that I got the chance to meet Greg, Ray, and the rest of the team.
